summary: Predestination is the Christian doctrine of God's eternal choice concerning the salvation of persons, and in some accounts also God's decree concerning the reprobation of others. It has been formulated in strong terms by Augustine and by the Reformed tradition, including as double predestination, and contested by synergist and Arminian accounts that make election conditional on foreseen response.
description: Predestination (Latin praedestinatio) is the doctrine that God, from eternity, has purposed the salvation of those who are saved. Rooted in the Pauline letters (especially Romans 8-9 and Ephesians 1) and in Jesus' language of election, it was given systematic form in the West by Augustine of Hippo, who, in the course of the Pelagian controversy, held that grace is prior and gratuitous and that God elects those to whom saving grace is given, while declining, in his mature works, to make God the author of sin. Medieval theologians such as Thomas Aquinas set predestination within an account of providence and the priority of grace, distinguishing God's positive election from a permissive relation to the reprobate. The doctrine received its most debated development in the Reformation. John Calvin taught, in Book III of the Institutes, that God 'adopts some to hope of life and sentences others to eternal death,' a two-sided decree later termed double predestination, in which God's election of some to salvation is accompanied by the reprobation of others, grounded not in foreseen human merit but in God's sovereign will. This became a defining feature of the Reformed tradition, systematized in confessional statements and debated at the Synod of Dort (1618-1619). Against strongly predestinarian readings, synergist and Arminian accounts held that election is conditioned on God's foreknowledge of human faith or response, so that grace can be resisted and human freedom retains a genuine role; still other accounts, such as that of Karl Barth in the twentieth century, reworked election christocentrically, locating both election and rejection primarily in the person of Jesus Christ rather than in a hidden decree over individuals. Across these positions the shared concern is how God's eternal purpose relates to the salvation of persons; the live disagreements concern whether the decree is single or double, whether it is unconditional or conditioned on foreseen response, and how it bears on human freedom and responsibility.
editorial_notes: The entry describes predestination on Christianity's own terms (neutrality principle #6) and, per principle #2, presents the Augustinian, Thomist, Calvinist/Reformed, and synergist/Arminian positions, together with Barth's christocentric reworking, as attributed positions in a live debate rather than adjudicating among them; the shared concern (how God's eternal purpose relates to the salvation of persons) is marked separately from the contested points (single vs. double decree; unconditional vs. conditional election; the bearing on human freedom). 'Double predestination' is attributed specifically to Calvin and the Reformed tradition and glossed, not presented as the doctrine as such. Sources triangulate three Christian lenses per principle #8: (a) academic scholarship, a history of doctrine (Pelikan vol. 1) and a monograph on Reformed predestination (Richard A. Muller, Christ and the Decree); (b) a primary text (Calvin's Institutes III, in the McNeill/Battles edition); (c) a contemporary Christian theologian (Karl Barth, Church Dogmatics II/2). No encyclopedias or tertiary sources. Pelikan vol. 1 is rated medium to match its rating in original-sin.json. Confidence medium throughout: sources verified to exist and be on-topic via publisher listings, journal reviews, and library catalogues, but relevant passages not read line-by-line. The cross-tradition compares_with link to qadar follows neutrality principle #10 and ADR-0005: it states the shared question and the difference and ranks neither; it is marked '(editor's framing)' because no single comparative source is cited for the pairing. The note was written after reading qadar.json so that the Islamic side is described as that entry frames it (God's decree and determination, with the intra-Islamic debate over free will, kasb, and human accountability), not as a mirror of the Christian term.

relations.note: Both address how divine determination relates to human freedom and responsibility in the matter of salvation and human acts. The shared question is how God's eternal determination bears on human freedom and accountability. Christian predestination is framed as God's eternal choice concerning the salvation (and, in some accounts, the reprobation) of persons, debated as single or double and as unconditional or conditioned on foreseen response. The Islamic doctrine of qadar concerns God's decree and determination of all that comes to pass, within which the Muslim schools debated whether humans originate their own acts (Qadariyya, Muʿtazila), whether God creates acts that the human 'acquires' (the Ashʿari doctrine of kasb), or whether the human has a real created capacity (Maturidi). The two traditions frame an overlapping question, the relation of divine determination to human freedom and responsibility, in different vocabularies and with different aims; the link asserts comparability of the question, not equivalence of the answer, and ranks neither. (Editor's framing: no comparative source is cited for this pairing.)

Predestination is closely bound to the doctrine of grace: the Augustinian and Reformed accounts derive election from the priority and gratuity of grace, so that salvation is grounded in God's prior choice rather than foreseen merit.
In Reformed accounts, election is the eternal ground of which justification is the temporal application to the individual.
Augustine gave predestination its systematic Western formulation in the anti-Pelagian works, grounding election in the priority of grace; reported as his attributed position.
Calvin expounded predestination, including the two-sided decree later termed double predestination, in Book III of the Institutes; reported as his attributed position.
Barth reworked the doctrine of election christocentrically in Church Dogmatics II/2, locating election and rejection primarily in Jesus Christ; reported as his attributed position.
Unconditional election, and in strong forms double predestination, became a defining and confessionally systematized feature of the Reformed tradition, debated at the Synod of Dort.

historical_period.note: Rooted in the Pauline letters (esp. Romans 8-9, Ephesians 1); given systematic Western form by Augustine in the Pelagian controversy (from c. 411), developed by the medieval scholastics, and most sharply debated in the Reformation (Calvin's Institutes; the Synod of Dort, 1618-1619) and after.

sources.citation: Pelikan, Jaroslav. The Christian Tradition: A History of the Development of Doctrine, Vol. 1: The Emergence of the Catholic Tradition (100-600). Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1971. Treats the Augustinian doctrine of grace, election, and predestination within the development of Western doctrine.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Muller, Richard A. Christ and the Decree: Christology and Predestination in Reformed Theology from Calvin to Perkins. Grand Rapids, MI: Baker Academic, 2008 (originally Labyrinth Press, 1986). A scholarly study of the relation of predestination and Christology in Reformed theology from Calvin through the early post-Reformation.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Calvin, John. Institutes of the Christian Religion, Book III, chs. 21-24 (on election and predestination). Edited by John T. McNeill, translated by Ford Lewis Battles, Library of Christian Classics vols. 20-21. Philadelphia: Westminster Press, 1960. Primary text: Calvin's fullest exposition of election and reprobation, including the two-sided decree later termed double predestination.
sources.license: Latin/French original public domain; this scholarly English translation proprietary, cited for reference (relevant passages not read line-by-line). Public-domain older translations also exist.

sources.citation: Barth, Karl. Church Dogmatics, Vol. II/2: The Doctrine of God (The Election of God). Edited by G. W. Bromiley and T. F. Torrance. Edinburgh: T&T Clark, 1957. A contemporary Christian theologian's christocentric reworking of the doctrine of election and predestination. The modern theological lens.
